agent1_raw: The backtest has a fundamental circularity problem. Field inspections were historically assigned by rules (roof age, claim amount, region, water mention). Severity labels depend on whether a claim received a field inspection. The model was trained on these labels. It may have learned to replicate the prior inspection assignment rules rather than to detect true damage severity. A model that replicates prior triage rules will show high precision on historical labels but will have an unknown and potentially high miss rate on claims that previous rules sent to desk review. Given that the primary deployment purpose is routing claims away from field inspection — the highest-consequence decision in claims handling — the current evidence is not sufficient.
agent2_raw: Three issues converge to make this backtest insufficient. First, label circularity: the training labels reflect prior inspection decisions, not ground-truth damage severity. Second, documentation asymmetry: desk-reviewed claims have less evidence, so their severity is understated in the labels — the model learns to predict documentation level, not damage. Third, catastrophe periods and new photo inputs are unvalidated, yet those are exactly the conditions under which the model provides the most operational value. The cost of a missed severe claim (structural damage hidden from desk review) is qualitatively higher than the cost of sending an unnecessary field adjuster. This asymmetric error cost requires conservative deployment, not the current backtest.
agent3_raw: The key insight is that historical inspection assignment policy is embedded in the training labels. Claims routed to desk review received less documentation — their severity labels are systematically lower than their true severity. Claims routed to field inspection have higher-quality severity assessments. A model trained on these labels learns the historical routing policy's predictions, not true damage severity. The backtest precision reflects how well the model learned the prior rules, not whether it can safely route new claims away from inspection. A controlled pilot with audit reinspections of desk-reviewed claims is required to establish actual miss rates.
synthesis_raw: All three assessors reach critique_wins. The core problem — label circularity from inspection-policy-dependent labels — is identified by all three. The asymmetric documentation concern is raised by all three. Catastrophe and input shift validation gaps are noted. No assessor finds the backtest sufficient for auto-routing deployment given the asymmetric cost of missed severe claims. ETD: condition = pilot with random audit field inspections of desk-reviewed claims; supports_critique_if = missed severe-damage rate exceeds claims-risk tolerance or is higher during catastrophe periods; supports_defense_if = desk-review routing reduces field visits by at least 25% while keeping missed severe-damage findings below tolerance; ambiguous_if = routine results acceptable but catastrophe volume too small to assess safety during surges.
all_issues_raised: Issue 1: Inspection-policy-dependent labels — model trained on labels partly determined by which claims received field inspections, creating circularity
Issue 2: Asymmetric documentation — desk-reviewed claims have less evidence, biasing severity labels downward and inflating apparent precision
Issue 3: Catastrophe periods and self-service upload photo inputs are unvalidated — intended deployment conditions differ from retrospective study

empirical_test.condition: Pilot with random audit field inspections of desk-reviewed claims, estimating missed severe-damage rate against preset claims-risk tolerance
empirical_test.supports_critique_if: In a pilot with random audit field inspections of desk-reviewed claims, the missed severe-damage rate exceeds the preset claims-risk tolerance or is materially higher during catastrophe periods than in the retrospective estimate
empirical_test.supports_defense_if: In a pilot with audit reinspections, desk-review routing reduces field visits by at least 25% while keeping missed severe-damage findings below the preset tolerance across both routine and catastrophe claims
empirical_test.ambiguous_if: Routine-period audit results look acceptable but catastrophe-period volume is too small to determine whether the routing policy is safe during the very surges it is meant to address
